narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students who came before you. Your Year 1 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at your school, which stood out as the biggest signal of concern—it painted a picture of early struggles that often make the steep climb to bar success feel even tougher. Building on that, your Year 3 grades also landed in the 2nd decile, reinforcing a consistent challenge in keeping pace with the pack, even as law school demands ramp up. Your LSAT score of 36 added to this narrative, suggesting the analytical hurdles of the bar might prove similarly demanding. With your undergraduate GPA at 2.5 and coming from a lower-middle family income bracket, the model saw a profile where resources and academic momentum have historically been harder to sustain for passing the bar on the first try. Factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies wove in as subtle but aligning threads, all tipping the scales toward a 56.9% predicted chance of not passing. It's not that any one piece defined you, but together they told a story of an uphill battle the model couldn't confidently bet on. This is why your application was rejected—it's a tough call meant to protect spots for those whose stories align more closely with bar success. The good news? You're full of potential, and focusing on boosting those early grades or LSAT prep could rewrite your story entirely. You've got this if you tackle those core areas head-on. Keep pushing forward.
